title: Entropy and Gibbs Free Energy
subject: AP Chemistry
objectives: Define entropy as a measure of disorder/dispersal and predict the sign of ΔS for a reaction
Apply the Gibbs equation ΔG = ΔH − TΔS to determine whether a reaction is spontaneous
Analyze the four ΔH/ΔS sign combinations and their implications for spontaneity

sections.heading: Entropy: The Universe Tends Toward Disorder
sections.content:
Enthalpy tells you about heat flow, but it does not tell the full story of why reactions happen. Entropy (S) is the other half. Entropy is a measure of the dispersal of energy and matter — the number of ways particles and energy can be arranged (microstates).

The Second Law of Thermodynamics states that the total entropy of the universe always increases in a spontaneous process. Nature favors spreading out: gas expands to fill a container, hot objects cool to room temperature, sugar dissolves in water. All of these increase the total number of microstates.

You can predict the sign of ΔS (entropy change) for a reaction using simple rules:
• ΔS > 0 (entropy increases): Solids → liquids → gases, fewer moles → more moles of gas, dissolving a solid in water
• ΔS < 0 (entropy decreases): Gases → liquids → solids, more moles → fewer moles of gas, crystallization

sections.heading: Predicting ΔS from Reaction Equations
sections.content:
Reaction 1: 2H₂O(l) → 2H₂(g) + O₂(g)
  ΔS > 0 (liquid → gas, 0 moles gas → 3 moles gas — huge entropy increase)

Reaction 2: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) → 2NH₃(g)
  ΔS < 0 (4 moles gas → 2 moles gas — fewer microstates for gas particles)

Reaction 3: NaCl(s) → Na⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq)
  ΔS > 0 (ordered crystal → ions dispersed in solution)

The key is counting gas moles and looking at phase changes. Gas molecules have far more entropy than liquids, which have more than solids.

sections.heading: Gibbs Free Energy: The Spontaneity Criterion
sections.content:
Gibbs free energy (G) combines enthalpy and entropy into a single criterion for spontaneity at constant temperature and pressure:

ΔG = ΔH − TΔS

If ΔG < 0: The reaction is spontaneous (thermodynamically favorable) — it can proceed without continuous external energy input.
If ΔG > 0: The reaction is nonspontaneous — it requires continuous energy input.
If ΔG = 0: The system is at equilibrium.

Notice the minus sign in front of TΔS. This means that nature favors both lowering enthalpy (exothermic, ΔH < 0) AND increasing entropy (ΔS > 0). A reaction that does both is always spontaneous. A reaction that does neither is never spontaneous.
ΔG = ΔH − TΔS determines spontaneity. ΔG < 0 means spontaneous. Spontaneity depends on both the enthalpy change (energy) and entropy change (disorder), weighted by temperature.

sections.heading: The Four Sign Combinations
sections.content:
The interplay of ΔH and ΔS creates four possibilities:

1. ΔH < 0, ΔS > 0: ΔG is ALWAYS negative. Spontaneous at all temperatures.
   Example: Combustion reactions — exothermic and produce more gas molecules.

2. ΔH > 0, ΔS < 0: ΔG is ALWAYS positive. Never spontaneous.
   The reverse of case 1. These reactions require continuous energy input.

3. ΔH < 0, ΔS < 0: ΔG depends on temperature. Spontaneous at LOW temperatures (where the ΔH term dominates) but not at high temperatures (where TΔS overwhelms ΔH).
   Example: Freezing water — exothermic but decreases entropy. Spontaneous below 0°C.

4. ΔH > 0, ΔS > 0: ΔG depends on temperature. Spontaneous at HIGH temperatures (where TΔS outweighs ΔH) but not at low temperatures.
   Example: Melting ice — endothermic but increases entropy. Spontaneous above 0°C.

The crossover temperature (where ΔG = 0) is T = ΔH/ΔS. This is the temperature at which the system is at equilibrium between the two states — exactly the boiling point or melting point for phase changes.

aiContext: AP Chemistry. Use dimensional analysis for all calculations. Stress WHY reactions happen, not just equation balancing. Common confusion: entropy vs enthalpy direction, and applying Le Chatelier to specific perturbations.
